stimulus: An archaeological team led by Sara Bremmer and Andrei Volkov found fragments of a 3,500-year-old polished-bone tool at a Bronze Age site in southern Finland. Little remains of the implement except a slender bone shaft with characteristic grooves along one edge. Some scholars argue that the tool was largely used by long-distance herders.
stem: Which finding, if true, would most directly support the scholars’ argument?

answerOptions.content: Other tools dating to the same period have been found in the remains of several dwellings in the region, including in one that may have belonged to a herder.
Remains of other tool types have also been found at the Finnish site that probably reached the location through trade with herding peoples.
Similar tools have been found at other sites of the same period that were connected to the Finnish site via known seasonal grazing corridors.
The other known examples of this tool from the same period have all been found along routes that seem to have been used primarily by herders moving livestock between pastures.

rationale: Choice D is the best answer because it links the tool’s known distribution directly to herder routes—a strong inference that the implement was used by long-distance herders specifically. Choice A places one tool in one potentially-herder dwelling. Choice B describes other goods, not the tool. Choice C describes connection of sites along grazing corridors but doesn’t establish primary use by herders.
